introduction:
Aggregate functions in SQL are used to perform calculations on a set of rows and return a single value. 
      These functions are essential for data analysis and reporting as they allow you to summarize large amounts 
      of data efficiently. Aggregate functions ignore NULL values in their calculations.

sections.title: COUNT() Function
sections.content:
The COUNT() function returns the number of rows that match a specified condition. It can be used with 
        COUNT(*) to count all rows, or COUNT(column_name) to count non-NULL values in a specific column.

sections.title: SUM() Function
sections.content:
The SUM() function calculates the total sum of a numeric column. It is commonly used to find totals 
        such as total sales, total salary, etc.

sections.title: AVG() Function
sections.content:
The AVG() function calculates the average value of a numeric column. It returns the mean of all 
        non-NULL values in the specified column.

sections.title: MIN() Function
sections.content:
The MIN() function returns the minimum value from a column. It works with numeric, date, and 
        string data types.

sections.title: MAX() Function
sections.content:
The MAX() function returns the maximum value from a column. Similar to MIN(), it works with 
        various data types.

sections.title: GROUP BY Clause
sections.content:
The GROUP BY clause groups rows that have the same values in specified columns into summary rows. 
        It is used with aggregate functions to group the result-set by one or more columns.

havingClause.title: HAVING Clause
havingClause.content:
The HAVING clause is used to filter groups after the GROUP BY clause has been applied. Unlike the 
      WHERE clause, which filters rows before grouping, HAVING filters groups based on aggregate function results.
havingClause.code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) 
FROM employees 
GROUP BY department 
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;

points.description: Filters rows before grouping and aggregation
Filters groups after grouping and aggregation

practicalExample.title: Practical Example
practicalExample.description: Consider a table employees with columns: id, name, department, salary
practicalExample.code:
-- Find average salary per department with more than 5 employees
SELECT department, AVG(salary) as avg_salary, COUNT(*) as emp_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

realWorldApplication.title: YouTube Analytics Dashboard
realWorldApplication.scenario: Imagine a YouTuber looking at their dashboard. They don't want to see a list of 5 million individual video clicks. They want to know their total views (`SUM`), average watch time (`AVG`), and most popular video (`MAX`). The database uses `GROUP BY` to aggregate millions of rows into a clean summary by Month or by Video.
